thinks.think: A clear glass bowl sits centrally on a light wood-grain table. In the upper left corner, a person's hand enters the frame, reaching towards the edge of the table near a small, partially visible object. The bowl remains stationary throughout the clip.

A hand enters from the left holding a white measuring cup containing liquid. The cup is held directly above the clear glass bowl. A white text box appears in the upper left corner displaying the text '1/4 warm water' in a black serif font.

The hand tilts the white measuring cup, pouring the clear liquid into the glass bowl until the cup is empty. After pouring, the hand moves the empty cup away to the left side of the frame. The text overlay "1/4 warm water" disappears from the screen as the action completes.

A hand presents a white plastic bottle featuring a blue and orange label directly over the glass bowl. Text on the label identifies it as "ELMER'S Washable School Glue" and "Colle d'école". The empty white measuring cup lies on the wooden table surface in the upper left background.

Two hands grasp the white plastic bottle labeled as school glue. The hands manipulate the top of the bottle, appearing to unscrew or open the cap while positioning the container directly over the glass bowl filled with water. The white measuring cup remains resting on the wooden surface in the upper left background.

Hands grip the white bottle, tilting it steeply over the glass bowl. The bottle rotates to show a barcode on the back as the opening positions directly above the water. The white measuring cup remains stationary on the wooden table surface in the background.

A hand tilts a large white bottle, pouring thick white liquid into a white measuring cup held directly over the clear glass bowl. A text overlay reading '1/8 cup School glue' appears in the bottom left corner against the light wood-grain table surface.

A hand pours white glue from a large bottle into a white measuring cup held over the glass bowl. The text '1/8 cup School glue' remains visible in the bottom left corner. The white liquid flows steadily from the bottle into the cup, which is positioned directly above the water-filled bowl.

A hand wearing a ring pours thick white liquid from a large white bottle into a white measuring cup held over a glass bowl. The cup fills with the viscous substance. The text overlay is absent. The background remains a light wood-grain table surface.

A hand tilts the white measuring cup to pour the thick white glue into the glass bowl filled with water. A text box appears in the upper right corner reading "If you want a bigger batch just double". The glue flows out of the cup and begins to mix with the water at the bottom of the bowl.

A hand continues pouring thick white glue from a measuring cup into the glass bowl. The viscous liquid forms a growing white pool at the bottom. The text 'If you want a bigger batch just double' stays in the upper right, and the large glue bottle sits on the left.

The text overlay disappears as the white glue finishes pouring from the measuring cup into the water. A solid white mass of glue rests at the bottom of the bowl. A hand enters the frame on the left, resting near a silver fork on the wooden surface.

summary.text: [0-2] A clear glass bowl sits centrally on a light wood-grain table while a hand reaches in the upper left. [2-6] A hand holds a white measuring cup containing liquid above the bowl as text '1/4 warm water' appears. The hand pours the clear liquid into the bowl until empty, then moves the cup left as the text vanishes. [6-8] A hand presents a white plastic bottle with a blue and orange label over the bowl; label text reads "ELMER'S Washable School Glue" and "Colle d'école". The empty measuring cup lies on the table. [8-12] Two hands grasp the glue bottle, unscrewing the cap over the bowl. They tilt the bottle steeply, showing a barcode, positioning the opening above the water while the measuring cup remains stationary.

[14-18] A hand pours white glue from a large white bottle into a white measuring cup held over a glass bowl filled with water. The text "1/8 cup School glue" is displayed in the bottom left. The cup fills with the thick, viscous white liquid.
[18-24] The hand tilts the measuring cup to pour the glue into the bowl. A text overlay appears reading "If you want a bigger batch just double". The white glue flows out, forming a growing white pool at the bottom of the clear water. The large bottle rests on the table to the left.
[24-28] After pouring, a hand uses a silver fork to stir the white glue blob inside the glass bowl. Another hand holds the rim steady. The text "What is Gesso?" appears in the top left corner as the fork tines penetrate and agitate the white substance within the water.

The text box reading 'What is Gesso?' disappears from the screen. Hands continue stirring a white glue and water mixture inside a clear glass bowl using a silver fork on a light wood-grain surface. The white substance disperses further into the clear liquid, creating a cloudy, milky consistency as the fork agitates the contents vigorously. The bowl remains stationary. A cartoon graphic of a green dollar bill with small yellow hands suddenly appears in the bottom right corner. This graphic transforms, now sporting white wings on its sides instead of hands, while the stirring continues until the liquid appears fully blended.

[40-42] Hands stir milky white mixture with silver fork; green dollar bill graphic vanishes. [42-44] Fork placed left of bowl; hand holds red/white "CORN STARCH" box featuring a pot of vegetables. [44-46] "Plaster of Paris" inset appears left; hand holds white bottle over bowl. [46-48] Hand sets down "Johnson's baby powder" bottle behind bowl; "CORN STARCH" box stands right. [48-50] Inset vanishes; hand lifts "CORN STARCH" box while baby powder remains behind bowl.

[52-64] A hand holds a white measuring spoon filled with white powder over a glass bowl containing milky liquid, while another hand stirs the mixture with a silver fork. A red and white box labeled "CORN STARCH" stands upright in the background on the light wood table. The person tilts the spoon to add the powder into the bowl, continuing to stir vigorously with the fork. By the end, the spoon is emptied into the mixture as the ingredients combine.

From 64-66s, the right hand grips the glass bowl rim for stability while the left hand vigorously stirs the white mixture with a silver fork. A red and white corn starch box stands upright in the background on the light wood table. At 66-68s, a hand positions a white measuring spoon filled with white powder over the bowl. Between 68-72s, the spoon tilts to pour the powder into the milky liquid as the other hand stirs vigorously with the fork. By 72-74s, the spoon is held over the bowl while the fork continues mixing the opaque white substance.

From [74-76], a white measuring spoon is placed on the light wooden table to the left of a clear glass bowl while a hand stirs a thick white liquid inside using a silver fork. Throughout [76-80], the hand vigorously mixes the opaque white mixture as the white plastic measuring spoon remains stationary on the surface. A red and white corn starch box stands upright in the background, showing a colorful dish. Finally, at [80-82], a white text box appears in the upper right reading "This batch took 4 Tablespoons" as the stirring continues.

[82-84] The white text overlay stating 'This batch took 4 Tablespoons' disappears from the upper right area. A hand continues to stir the opaque white mixture within the clear glass bowl using a silver fork. The hand pours the clear liquid into the bowl until empty, then moves the cup left as the text vanishes. [6-8] A hand presents a white plastic bottle with a blue and orange label over the bowl; label text reads "ELMER'S Washable School Glue" and "Colle d'école". The empty measuring cup lies on the table. [8-12] Two hands grasp the glue bottle, unscrewing the cap over the bowl. They tilt the bottle steeply, showing a barcode, positioning the opening above the water while the measuring cup remains stationary.

[14-18] A hand pours white glue from a large white bottle into a white measuring cup held over a glass bowl filled with water. The text "1/8 cup School glue" is displayed in the bottom left. The cup fills with the thick, viscous white liquid.
[18-24] The hand tilts the measuring cup to pour the glue into the bowl. A text overlay appears reading "If you want a bigger batch just double". The white glue flows out, forming a growing white pool at the bottom of the clear water. The large bottle rests on the table to the left.
[24-28] After pouring, a hand uses a silver fork to stir the white glue blob inside the glass bowl. Another hand holds the rim steady. The text "What is Gesso?" appears in the top left corner as the fork tines penetrate and agitate the white substance within the water.

The text box reading 'What is Gesso?' disappears from the screen. Hands continue stirring a white glue and water mixture inside a clear glass bowl using a silver fork on a light wood-grain surface. The white substance disperses further into the clear liquid, creating a cloudy, milky consistency as the fork agitates the contents vigorously. The bowl remains stationary. A cartoon graphic of a green dollar bill with small yellow hands suddenly appears in the bottom right corner. This graphic transforms, now sporting white wings on its sides instead of hands, while the stirring continues until the liquid appears fully blended.

[40-42] Hands stir milky white mixture with silver fork; green dollar bill graphic vanishes. [42-44] Fork placed left of bowl; hand holds red/white "CORN STARCH" box featuring a pot of vegetables. [44-46] "Plaster of Paris" inset appears left; hand holds white bottle over bowl. [46-48] Hand sets down "Johnson's baby powder" bottle behind bowl; "CORN STARCH" box stands right. [48-50] Inset vanishes; hand lifts "CORN STARCH" box while baby powder remains behind bowl.

[52-64] A hand holds a white measuring spoon filled with white powder over a glass bowl containing milky liquid, while another hand stirs the mixture with a silver fork. A red and white box labeled "CORN STARCH" stands upright in the background on the light wood table. The person tilts the spoon to add the powder into the bowl, continuing to stir vigorously with the fork. By the end, the spoon is emptied into the mixture as the ingredients combine.

recent_thinks.text: A hand tilts a large white bottle, pouring thick white liquid into a white measuring cup held directly over the clear glass bowl. A text overlay reading '1/8 cup School glue' appears in the bottom left corner against the light wood-grain table surface.

A hand holds up a red and white box labeled "CORN STARCH" showing a pot of vegetables. The red "Plaster of Paris" inset image on the left and the white baby powder bottle behind the bowl have disappeared. A silver fork lies next to the glass bowl with white liquid.

The white measuring spoon is no longer held over the bowl as the right hand moves to grip the glass rim for stability. The left hand continues to vigorously stir the white mixture inside the bowl using a silver fork. The red and white corn starch box remains standing upright in the background on the light wood surface.

A hand holds a white measuring spoon filled with white powder over the glass bowl, while another hand grips a silver fork inside the milky mixture. The red and white corn starch box remains standing in the background on the light wood table surface. The spoon is positioned above the bowl to add more powder.

A hand tilts a white measuring spoon filled with white powder, pouring it into the glass bowl containing the milky liquid. Simultaneously, the other hand uses a silver fork to stir the mixture vigorously as the powder is added. The corn starch box remains standing in the background on the wooden table.

The jar, which is already partially filled with the white mixture, features a label reading "HOMEMADE GESSO" in black capital letters. Throughout the sequence, the spoon moves along the bowl's interior to gather the remaining mixture, ensuring the transfer of the substance from the bowl into the container is completed. The focus remains on the manual transfer process using the utensil to move the gesso.
